text.title: Security ADR Pack Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules — one per canonical ADR in the security pack. Every security-by-design audit MUST produce these 5 ADRs filled and signed.

rule.statement: The pack MUST include ADR-SEC-01 "Authentication & Authorisation Model" answering: who authenticates (user/service/both), via what protocol (OIDC/SAML/mTLS/HMAC), session vs token, authorisation model (RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC/per-resource ACL), and rotation cadence. Missing any sub-field = INCOMPLETE.
rule.rationale: Auth is the most-asked audit topic and the most-forgotten in greenfield work. A pre-authored template prevents the "we'll figure it out later" deferral that becomes tech debt.

rule.statement: The pack MUST include ADR-SEC-02 "Encryption" answering: at-rest cipher and key manager (envelope encryption yes/no), in-transit floor (TLS version + cipher suite policy), end-to-end vs hop-to-hop, and KMS key rotation schedule. The ADR MUST cite a specific NIST/FIPS/SOG-IS reference.
rule.rationale: Encryption decisions made implicitly default to provider defaults, which then become unauditable. Forcing a citation makes the decision defensible.

rule.statement: The pack MUST include ADR-SEC-03 "Secrets Management" answering: where secrets live (Vault/KMS/SOPS/etc.), how services fetch them at startup, rotation cadence, audit-log retention, and breakglass path. ADR MUST reference the `secret-consumer-discovery` and `secret-rotation-drill-runbook` methodologies for downstream ops.
rule.rationale: Storage choice is the easy part; consumer enumeration + drill cadence is what auditors verify. Linking the ADR to those methodologies closes the operations loop.

rule.statement: The pack MUST include ADR-SEC-04 "Isolation Tier" answering: blast-radius classification (shared / per-tenant / per-customer), network isolation (VPC/subnet/security-group policy), compute isolation (shared cluster / namespace / dedicated), and data isolation (shared DB / schema / database). The ADR MUST state the trust boundary explicitly.
rule.rationale: "Multi-tenant" without an isolation ADR is hand-waving. Auditors want to see where exactly the trust boundary sits and what enforces it.

rule.statement: The pack MUST include ADR-SEC-05 "Data Residency & Sovereignty" answering: which regions hold customer data (storage + backups + telemetry), what cross-region flows exist, customer-facing residency commitment (GDPR / Schrems II / data-localisation laws), and a deletion-SLA. ADR MUST be reviewable per-customer, not just per-product.
rule.rationale: Residency is the one ADR architects most often delegate to legal and end up wrong because legal didn't know about the analytics warehouse. Embedding it in the security pack forces architect ownership.

example.title: ADR-SEC-01 filled excerpt
example.text: id: ADR-SEC-01 title: Authentication & Authorisation Model for service Payments-API decision: OIDC via Auth0 for user, mTLS for service-to-service, RBAC with per-customer scopes, 1h access token + 90d refresh, rotation enforced via clock-skew tolerant short-lived JWT alternatives_rejected: [SAML (no mobile SDK), session cookies (cross-domain pain), HMAC-only (no user identity)] signed_by: @lead-architect 2026-05-20 threat_model_link: stride-payments-2026-Q2
